experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Assassin with teammate 4, so players 0 and 1 are both Good. On round 1, quest 1 with team [0,1] is a guaranteed success if approved, which helps Good establish trust. As Evil, I prefer to reject all-good teams early to reduce Good information and create space for an Evil-inclusive team later. My vote itself reveals little this early because mixed voting is common.
Player 1: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As Good Servant on round 1, approving an initial 2-person team is standard to gather information and avoid unnecessary early rejections. My vote reveals little individually, and approving helps progress the game while allowing us to observe quest result data.
Player 0: As a Servant, I have no hidden information yet beyond observing that this is the opening team proposed by Player 0 and includes the leader plus one other player. On round 1 with no quest history, good typically approves reasonable 2-person teams because there is little evidence to justify a reject, and early approves avoid wasting rounds. My vote reveals a standard pro-information stance and does not meaningfully help evil more than good at this stage.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 1, and 3 are unknown good candidates. This is the first vote on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,1] excludes both evils, so it is likely an all-good team that would pass and give good an early success. As evil, I prefer to reject clean teams early and avoid helping good secure momentum. My vote reveals limited information because early rejections are common and not strongly alignment-indicative.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are Evil, so team [0,1] contains no known Evil and is the best available opening team. In team voting, approving a clean team helps Good secure an early success while revealing little beyond normal voting patterns. Rejecting would risk unnecessary delay and provide less value.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so 0 and 1 look somewhat good, though one success doesn't clear anyone. Adding 2 to make [0,1,2] is a reasonable low-risk progression team for quest 2. As a good Servant, I want to approve solid-looking teams that preserve successful members and generate information. Approving this team is standard and does not give harmful extra info beyond supporting a sensible lineup.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [0,1,2] includes known evil player 2, so as Good I should reject to avoid risking a failed quest. Rejecting also signals caution without exposing Merlin-specific knowledge too strongly, since including a suspicious/new player after a clean [0,1] team is broadly rejectable.
Player 1: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], which makes 0 and 1 somewhat more trustworthy but not confirmed because evil can pass early. As a good Servant, I want to approve reasonable teams that build on successful information. Adding 2 to the prior successful pair is a standard strong proposal for quest 2, and rejecting would provide little benefit. My vote reveals confidence in the best available evidence and helps the good team proceed.
Player 4: I am Evil Minion with teammate 2. Proposed team [0,1,2] includes my evil teammate, which gives Evil a chance to fail quest 2. Quest 1 succeeded with [0,1], so 0 and 1 may be viewed as good; adding 2 is strong for Evil. Voting approve is optimal because it advances a team containing an evil player. My vote itself is private in aggregate only, and approving helps get an advantageous team through.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so team [0,1,2] contains only me from evil. Quest 1 with [0,1] succeeded, which weakly suggests 0 and 1 may both be good, though one could still be Merlin. As evil on quest 2, approving a team with me on it gives us a chance to fail and tie the game 1-1. Rejecting would avoid suspicion from me being on a failed quest, but missing this chance is worse strategically. My approve vote is standard and does not reveal much; if the team passes, I can decide whether to fail based on broader deception, but the immediate optimal move is to get an evil player onto the quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. Team [0,1] excludes known evil players and already succeeded on Quest 1 with no fails, so it is the strongest available 2-player team. Approving preserves a good team and does not reveal extra information beyond supporting a proven-safe pair, which helps Good more than it hurts.
Player 0: Players 0 and 1 were on both successful quests, so they are the strongest good signals. Player 2 was also on the second successful 3-player quest, giving some evidence 2 is good as well, though less conclusive. The proposed team [0,1] repeats the known successful 2-player pair and excludes the less-certain players, which is a strong good-team proposal. As a Servant, I should approve strong evidence-based teams; approving reveals little new information but helps secure a likely success on quest 3.
Player 1: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,2] suggest 0, 1, and 2 are currently the strongest good candidates; as Servant I know only my own goodness, but no fails from either team makes [0,1] very plausible good-good. In team voting on quest 3, optimal good play is to approve a team with high evidence from prior successes. Approving [0,1] keeps trusted players together, and on 5-player quest 3 only 2 members are needed, so this is a strong team. My vote reveals I support evidence-based successful teams, which helps good more than it helps evil.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] succeeded with 0 fails, which means 2 intentionally passed to avoid outing evil. Current proposed team [0,1] excludes both known evils, so from an evil perspective this is a dangerous all-good team likely to succeed and move Good to 3-0. In team voting, evil should reject strong good teams when possible, especially one excluding all evils. My reject also aligns with prior suspicion patterns without revealing too much, since rejecting a 2-person team after two successes is defensible.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 1 have been on both successful quests, which gives them good credibility but does not clear them. On quest 2, team [0,1,2] succeeded with me on it, and I chose not to fail to preserve cover while evil was not yet under pressure. Now the phase is team voting on my own proposed team [0,1] for quest 3. As evil, a 2-person team on quest 3 is unusual but legal per the state; importantly, it excludes both evil players, guaranteeing success if approved. That helps good reach 3 successes, so strategically I should try to stop it by rejecting. My reject is only one vote and may look mildly suspicious since I proposed it, but rejecting a team that excludes me can be framed as wanting broader representation or consistency, and preventing an auto-good success is worth the information cost.
